name: Dobhar-chu
description: The Dobhar-chu, also known as the Irish Otterhound or King Otter, is a cryptid from Irish folklore. It is described as a large, dog-like otter creature said to inhabit lakes and rivers. Reputed to be incredibly aggressive and territorial, the Dobhar-chu is believed to be a fearsome predator. Sightings are rare, but stories of its vicious attacks have persisted for centuries, particularly in rural areas of Ireland. It is often portrayed as having armored skin, adding to its formidable reputation. The Dobhar-chu's cry is said to be a piercing shriek that warns of its impending attack. It is considered a cryptid because its existence has never been scientifically proven, relying on anecdotal evidence and folklore. The creature is often associated with death and misfortune, with many tales recounting encounters that end tragically for those who cross its path. Its powerful build and aquatic nature make it a dangerous foe in its natural habitat. The Dobhar-chu has captured the imagination and remains a chilling figure in Irish mythology, embodying the wild and untamed aspects of the Irish landscape. The lore surrounding it serves as a cautionary tale about respecting the power and potential dangers of the natural world.
subtitle: The Irish King Otter
appearance: The Dobhar-chu is described as a creature resembling a large otter or dog, often with dark fur and a streamlined body. Some accounts mention armored skin or scales, adding to its formidable appearance. Its size is typically larger than a regular otter, sometimes reaching the size of a large dog. It is described as having sharp teeth and claws, making it a dangerous predator. Its eyes are said to glow eerily in the dark.
habitat: The Dobhar-chu is said to inhabit lakes, rivers, and remote bodies of water throughout Ireland. It is often associated with secluded and wild areas, far from human habitation. It prefers areas with dense vegetation and rocky shores, providing cover and hunting grounds.
behavior: The Dobhar-chu is described as an aggressive and territorial creature. It is said to be fiercely protective of its territory and will attack anything that it perceives as a threat. It is primarily a nocturnal hunter, preying on fish, birds, and other animals that come within its reach. Its cry is a piercing shriek that is said to instill fear in those who hear it.

keyMyths.title: The Glenade Dobhar-chu Killing
keyMyths.description: The tale recounts a woman being attacked by a Dobhar-chu. Her husband killed the creature, but another Dobhar-chu, believed to be its mate, came after him seeking revenge.

sources.author: W.B. Yeats
sources.work: Fairy and Folk Tales of the Irish Peasantry
sources.description: Includes mentions of strange creatures and folklore, though not specifically Dobhar-chu in detail.
sources.text: Includes mentions of strange creatures and folklore, though not specifically Dobhar-chu in detail.
